coredata.dc:title: Shape memory hydrogel containing cucurbit[6]uril-carbon dots: Detection of metal ions in wastewater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yefang
Sun, Yahong
Fu, Zhiyao
Guo, Yu
Zhang, Dong
Huang, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: With the development of industry, more attention has been paid to the detection of metal ions in wastewater, especially with portable detection probes. Here, we prepared a multiple shape memory hydrogel containing cucurbit[6]uril-carbon dots, which can specifically recognize Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions. The self-assembly binding mode between CB[6] groups and carbon dots improves the structural stability of the hydrogel. The hydrogel is light yellow in natural light. It shows brown maple in the presence of Fe3+ ions and bright white in the presence of Hg2+ ions, indicating the adsorption of iron and mercury ions on the hydrogel. The fluorescence quenching of hydrogel is caused by ultraviolet competitive absorption of Fe3+ ions or Hg2+ ions, and the detection limit can reach 10−7 M. This visualized gel probe facilitates the development of portable detection devices. Meanwhile, the dynamic covalent borate bonds, acylhydrazone bonds and reversible crystallization of polyvinyl alcohol chains were used to achieve the shape transformation and excellent mechanical properties of the hydrogel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cucurbit[6]uril
Recognition
Fluorescence
Dynamic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recognition of metal ions by hydrogel

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Inner Mongolia Natural Science Foundation (No. 2021MS05002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of hydrogels at different sizes.

Triple shape memory based on borate bonds, acylhydrazone bonds and polyvinyl alcohol chains crystallization. (a, b) The fixation and restoration of the "V" gel shape is prepared by the formation and destruction of borate ester bonds; (c, d) The helical shape of hydrogel could be fixed and restored by the formation and destruction of dynamic acylhydrazone bonds; (e, f) the "V" hydrogel shape could be fixed and restored by freezing and thawing/heating.

The hydrogel was immersed in borax solution for different periods (2, 3, 4, 5 min) and then in acetic acid to record the process of shape memory and recovery. Initial state of hydrogel (a1, b1, c1, d1); hydrogel was soaked borax to obtain a temporary state (a2, b2, c2, d2); hydrogel was soaked acetic to restore the memory state (a5, b5, c5, d5).
(a) The excitation and emission spectra of CDs; (b) CIE chromaticity diagrams of CDs and hydrogel.

Under natural light (a) initial hydrogel; (b) hydrogel soaked in Fe2(SO4)3 solution and Hg(NO3)2 solution (c). Under 365 nm UV light (a′) initial fluorescence hydrogel; (b′) fluorescence quenching hydrogel soaked in Fe2(SO4)3 solution and Hg(NO3)2 solution (c′).
(a) Fluorescence intensity of hydrogel in different pH; (b) FT-IR spectra of hydrogel in different pH.

(a) The fluorescent response of hydrogel to Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions (b); (c) Hydrogel detection of metal ions (Cmetal ions = 300 μM); (d) Histogram of fluorescence intensity of Hg2+ in different metal ions; of Cd2+ (e); (f) Excitation of hydrogel and UV–vis spectra of metal ions.

(a) Initial hydrogel under natural light and (b); (c) The fluorescence of initial hydrogel under 365 nm UV light and (d); (e)The deformed hydrogel under natural light and (f); (g) The fluorescence of deformed hydrogel under natural light and (h); (i) The fluorescence of quenching with Fe3+ and Hg2+ (j); (k) Diagram of Sewer pipe model.

head.ce:title: Shape memory hydrogel containing cucurbit[6]uril-carbon dots: Detection of metal ions in wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot, 010021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the development of industry, more attention has been paid to the detection of metal ions in wastewater, especially with portable detection probes. Here, we prepared a multiple shape memory hydrogel containing cucurbit[6]uril-carbon dots, which can specifically recognize Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions. The self-assembly binding mode between CB[6] groups and carbon dots improves the structural stability of the hydrogel. The hydrogel is light yellow in natural light. It shows brown maple in the presence of Fe3+ ions and bright white in the presence of Hg2+ ions, indicating the adsorption of iron and mercury ions on the hydrogel. The fluorescence quenching of hydrogel is caused by ultraviolet competitive absorption of Fe3+ ions or Hg2+ ions, and the detection limit can reach 10−7 M. This visualized gel probe facilitates the development of portable detection devices. Meanwhile, the dynamic covalent borate bonds, acylhydrazone bonds and reversible crystallization of polyvinyl alcohol chains were used to achieve the shape transformation and excellent mechanical properties of the hydrogel.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hydrogel has multiple shape memory effects and can identify metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Cucurbit[n]urils (CB[n]), a class of macrocyclic hosts with rigid hydrophobic cavities and two identical carbonyl-fringed portals, have attracted much attention in supramolecular chemistry [1–3]. As a macrocyclic receptor molecule, cucurbit[6]uril (CB[6]), could be used to prepare luminescent materials by non-covalent interaction such as light-on/light-off switches [4,5], chemiluminescent hydrogels [6–8], Phototherapy drugs [9–12], fluorescence probe [13–15]. With the development of industry, environmental pollution is concerned and water pollution is the most urgent. Therefore, it is necessary to detect the water source. However, it will encounter harsh environment in the detection, which requires matching different shapes in different environments for detection. Hence, it is imperative to develop a deformable fluorescent probe.

Shape memory polymer is a kind of polymer material that can "remember" the temporary shape and restore the temporary shape to the initial shape through certain external stimulation, such as high temperature, freezing, acid and alkali [16–18]. In the past decade, shape memory polymers have attracted wide attention and have been applied in biomedical [19–21], sensors [22–24] and other research fields [25–27], with great potential application value. However, traditional shape memory polymers were usually heat-sensitive materials, which require high temperature to achieve their transformation from fixed temporary shape to restored initial shape. In order to make the application field of shape memory polymer more extensive, host-guest interaction, hydrogen bonding, metal coordination and dynamic covalent bonding were introduced into shape memory polymers, and these polymer materials were generally shape memory hydrogels [28–30]. Through the introduction of these valence bonds interactions, the prepared shape memory hydrogels had multiple memory effects. For example, Feng and his research group reported a hydrogel with multiple memory effects [31].

In this work, hydrogel was prepared by free radical polymerization and had multiple shape memory effects because of the dynamic covalent acylhydrazone bonds, borate bonds and crystallization of polyvinyl alcohol chains. At the same time, the unique cavity structure of cucurbit[6]uril was combined with the host-guest of carbon dots (CDs) to prepare supramolecular polymers, which could recognize Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions. The fluorescence quenching of hydrogel is caused by ultraviolet competitive absorption of Fe3+ ions or Hg2+ ions, and the detection limit can reach 10−7 M. It showed excellent mechanical properties before and after deformation. Hence, the visualized fluorescent probe was beneficial to the development and utilization of portable detection devices.

Methacryloyl hydrazide (MH) and cucurbit[6]uril-based monomer (CB[6]-MA) were synthesized according to the literature [32,33]. Acrylamide (MA), 4,4′-azobis (4-cyanovaleric acid) (ACVA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), N 'N-methylene diacrylamide (NNMD), glyoxal, Ethylenediamine, borax, citric acid and acetic acid were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals. All 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AVANCE III-500 NMR spectrometer. FT-IR spectra were obtained from nexus-6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were obtained from FL970 Techcomp fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence photographs of the hydrogel were observed at UV light excitations. UV–vis spectra were obtained using a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer.

Ethylenediamine (536 μL) and citric acid (0.46 g) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and reacted for 5 h at 200 °C in a 25 mL teflon high-pressure reactor. Then the NaOH (1 M) solution was slowly adjusted to pH = 14 and the color of the mixture changed from yellow-green to transparent brown-black. The crude product was obtained by low pressure distillation and dissolved in ethanol. The supernatant was centrifuged and evaporated to remove the ethanol. The solids were further dissolved in deionized water, filtered to remove the aggregated particles and frozen to obtain product (4 °C, 24 h).

MH (0.5 g), AM (0.34 g), PVA (0.5 g), CDs (10 mg), NNMD (6 mg) were added successively to a round bottom flask containing 10 mL deionized water. Stir continuously at 95 °C for 30 min to dissolve the solid particles completely. After lowering the temperature to 50 °C, CB[6]-MA (0.1 g) and initiator ACVA (0.056 g) were added to continue stirring. After 3 h, the reaction was continued in a specific mold for 3 h (60 °C) to obtain the memory hydrogel.

CDs were obtained by condensation ethylenediamine and citric under high temperature and pressure. 1H NMR spectra of CDs is shown in Fig. S1. 7.25–7.45 ppm was the proton peak corresponding to the benzene ring of CDs (1H). 3.95 ppm was the proton peak (2H) of the methylene group in -COOCH2-,3.65 ppm was the proton peak of methylene group in -CH2-OH (3H), 3.36 ppm was the proton peak in -CONH-CH2- (4H), 2.67–2.87 ppm was the proton peak in -N-CH2· and OC-CH2-C (5H). 2.01 ppm was the proton peak of the methyl group in -CC-CH3 (6H). 1.20 ppm was the proton peak of the methyl group in -C-CH3 (7H). The main peak of CDs was consistent with the literature [34], so it was successfully prepared. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured at the room temperature. At the excitation of 354 nm, CDs exhibited emission peak with the wavelengths of 427 nm (Fig. S2a). Around 8 nm size of the CDs was obtained by analysis of nanodynamometer (Table S1 and Fig. S2b). Table S2 shows that the mean zeta-potential of CDs is 0.14 mV after three tests.

CB[6]@CDs complex was prepared by the interaction between CB[6] and CDs through the host-guest. The binding behavior between CB[6] and CDs was represented by 1H NMR spectrum, as shown in Fig. S3. After adding CB[6], the 1H proton signal (7.25–7.45 ppm) moved to the high field by 1.6–1.7 ppm. The 7H proton signal (1.20 ppm) moved 0.45 ppm to the high field. These data indicated that some groups of CDs were inserted into the CB[6] cavity. In addition, 2H and 5H proton peaks were affected by the de-shielding effect of CB[6] port and moved to low field, and the corresponding signals 3.95 ppm (2H) and 2.67–2.87 (5H) were changed. As the proportion of CB[6] increases, the integrated area of 7H gradually increases, which also indicated that the CB[6]@CDs complex increased [35].

CB[6]-MA, MH, AM and PVA monomers were subjected to free radical polymerization in water and the cavity of CB[6] was used to combine with CDs to form hydrogel in the mold. Infrared spectra were performed to characterize various valence bonds in the hydrogel, as shown in Fig. S4. It was obvious carbonyl group of CB[6] characteristic peak at 1750 cm−1 for the hydrogel without CDs (Fig. S4b), and N-H deformation vibration peak of acrylamide at 1640 cm−1. The absorbance at 1610 cm−1 was derived from the N-N group of methylacrylamide hydrazide. By comparing curve b and c of Fig. S4, it is evident that the absorption peak of curve c at 1674 cm−1 is significantly wider, because CDs contain a large number of N-H bonds. Thus, the hydrogel with blue fluorescence was successfully prepared by the interaction between CDs and CB[6] units.

To explore the network structure inside the hydrogel, and Fig. 1 shows the SEM photos of the hydrogel freeze-dried and dehydrated. The gel has many porous structures because of the three-dimensional network structure formed between the monomer and the crosslinker. Therefore, the porous structure enhances the tensile properties of hydrogels to a certain extent.

Dynamic covalent bonds, multiple responsive, are particularly sensitive to external stimuli, which constructs smart polymer material, pH-responsive dynamic covalent bonds of acylhydrazone bonds and borate bonds were introduced in the system, resulting in the transformation of the gel from the initial shape to the temporary shape. The shape memory function of hydrogel was realized by three different interaction forces: formation and destruction of dynamic covalent borate bonds and acylhydrazone bonds and reversible crystallization of polyvinyl alcohol chains. The hydrogels were prepared to size of 50 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm and then bent the specimens and immerse it in borax solution (0.2 M) or glyoxal solution (0.2 M) to fix the temporary shape, respectively. The temporary shaped hydrogel was immersed in acetic acid to detect the stage of shape recovery. Temporary shapes were also fixed by freezing in the refrigerator for 2 h. However, they took a long time to thaw at room temperature, so thawing in deionized water at 60 °C to detect the stage of shape recovery, as shown in Fig. 2 The temporary shapes of the hydrogels were fixed by the interactions. Eliminating these interactions would restore the shape of the hydrogel to their original state.

Polyvinyl alcohol chains in hydrogel contained a large number of hydroxyl groups, which could react with borax solution to form dynamic covalent borate bonds. Therefore, the shapes of hydrogels in borax solution were fixed as "V" shape and were restored to their initial state in acetic acid ( Fig. 3). The reason was that dynamic covalent borate bonds breakage in acidic environment restored gels shape. To further investigate the effect of borate bonds on gels shape, we immersed gels in borax solution for different times and found that soaking time did not affect gels recovery to initial shape. In order to prove the change of valence bonds of hydrogels before and after deformation, infrared test was carried out (Fig. S5). Compared with the initial hydrogel, the peak of hydrogel@borate at 1670 cm−1 was significantly widened and the characteristic peak of BO at 1390 cm−1 was obvious, which indicated the formation of dynamic covalent borate bonds (Fig. S5c). After the gel was immersed in acetic acid (hydrogel@acetic acid), the peak at 1670 cm−1 narrowed and the peak at 1390 cm−1 disappeared, which was consistent with the characteristic peak of the initial gel (Fig. S5d). It had been shown that the dynamic covalent borate bonds were broken. The chemical structure of hydrogel@acetic acid was consistent with the initial gel and the shape was restored.

As shown in Fig. S6, linear hydrogels were immersed in glyoxal solution to fix their shape as "V". There were a large number of hydrazide groups in hydrogels, which could react with glyoxal to form dynamic covalent acylhydrazone bonds, thus fixing the shape of hydrogels. The restoration of the initial shape of fixed shape hydrogels in acetic acid was due to the fracture of the dynamic covalent acylhydrazone bonds in acidic environment. To further investigate the effect of acylhydrazone bonds on the shape of gels, these were immersed in glyoxal for different time. These were found that the time required for the hydrogel to recover to its initial shape increased when the soaking time was 4 min or more. This was because the number of reactions of hydrazine groups increased and the gel cross-linking density increased with the longer soaking time. As a result, it took more time to break the acylhydrazone bonds. The change of shape of hydrogels in glyoxal and acetic acid were essentially the formation and elimination of chemical bonds in hydrogels. Compared with the initial hydrogel, hydrogel@glyoxal showed a significant increase in peak intensity at 1661 cm−1 due to condensation of the aldehyde group with the hydrazide group to form CN bonds, indicating dynamic covalent acylhydrazone bonds formation (Fig. S7b). After the gel was immersed in glacial acid (hydrogel@glacial acetic), the peak intensity at 1661 cm−1 significantly weakened, which was consistent with the characteristic peak of the initial gel (Fig. S7c). It was previously shown that the dynamic covalent acylhydrazone bonds were broken. The chemical structure of hydrogel@glacial acetic was consistent with the initial gel and the shape was restored.

The prepared hydrogel had dynamic covalent acylhydrazone bonds, borate bonds and interpenetrating network of polyvinyl alcohol chain, which made the gel mechanically superior. The hydrogels were immersed in borax, glyoxal solution, and then in acetic acid solution to judge the mechanical properties of the gel before and after deformation. As shown in Fig. S8, when hydrogels were immersed in borax solution for 0, 2, 3,4 and 5 min, temporary shape formation and soaking time did not affect the maximum tension or reduce the tensile strength. According to Fig. 3, hydrogels could recover their shape within 2 min of immersion in acetic acid. Based on this, the above hydrogels were immersed in acetic acid solution for 2 min before detection. The tensile strength of the hydrogel after shape transformation was from 0.439 MPa to 0.397 MPa. The difference was 0.042 MPa (9 %), which was tiny and negligible (Fig. S8b). No obvious adverse effect on the mechanical properties of the gel before and after deformation. In addition, hydrogels were immersed in glyoxal solution for 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 min. According to Fig. S6, these hydrogels recovered their shape within 4 min of immersion in acetic acid. On this basis, the above hydrogels were immersed in acetic acid solution for 2–4 min before detection. The variation in tensile strength of hydrogels was negligible (30 %). It indicated that the mechanical properties of gels were not affected absolutely before and after deformation (Fig. S8d). The gel exhibited remarkable mechanical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of metal ions by hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: When excited at 341 nm, hydrogel present stronger band emission at 475 nm, which is similar to that of CDs ( Fig. 4a). Therefore, the emissions of hydrogel can be ascribed to the luminescence of CDs (Fig. 4b). The UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs possess two well-defined absorption peaks in the UV or visible region, As shown in Fig. S9a, two characteristic absorption peaks of CDs were at 234 nm and 328 nm (blank curve). The UV spectrum of hydrogel has a wide peak at 203–316 nm, which may be caused by complex formation (red curve).

1H NMR indicated that -CB[6] units were bound to CDs through host-guest interaction. In order to further clarify the role of -CB[6] group in gels, CB[6] functionalized hydrogels and hydrogels without CB[6] were prepared. The essential difference between these two hydrogels was that the binding modes of CDs in hydrogels were respectively supramolecular self-assembly binding of -CB[6] and ordinary physical adsorption. Two gels of the same quality were respectively immersed in a beaker containing 50 mL deionized water. After 48 h, the fluorescence of deionized water in the beaker were detected, as shown in Fig. S9. Soaking in -CB[6] units functionalized hydrogel, the fluorescence intensity of deionized water was much lower than that of deionized water soaked in hydrogel without -CB[6]. This indicated that the self-assembly binding mode of -CB[6] and CDs was far more compact and chemically stable than that of physical doping. hydrogel with -CB[6] could produce blue light under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. Encounter iron salt or mercury salt (0.2 mg/mL) fluorescence quenching, blue light disappeared ( Fig. 5). The hydrogel appeared pale yellow in natural light (Fig. 5a), and showed brown maple in the presence of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 5b) and showed bright white in the presence of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 5c), indicating the adsorption of iron and mercury ions on the hydrogel.

In order to understand the stability of CDs materials in different pH solutions, the fluorescence stability experiments of hydrogel were also carried out. It has found that the fluorescence intensity of hydrogel remains substantially stable in aqueous solutions with a pH range of 1–14 ( Fig. 6a), respectively; and the FT-IR spectra of hydrogel in pH 1–14 retain unchanged with a consistent structure (Fig. 6b). In summary, hydrogel has stable luminescence property and wide range of pH stability in aqueous solution.

The optimal experimental conditions were used to determine whether Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions could effectively quench the fluorescence of the hydrogel, and the linear response range and limit of detection (LOD) were detected. According to the Stern-Volmer equation: I0/I = 1 + KSV[Q]. Where I and I0 were respectively the fluorescence intensity of hydrogel with or without Fe3+ ions. Q was the Fe3+ ion concentration, and KSV is the constant of Stern-Volmer equation [34]. The fluorescence intensity at 475 nm decreased with the increase of Fe3+ ions concentration (0–1.25 mM). There was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ ions concentration (R2 = 0.957). The LOD of Fe3+ was calculated and verified to be 0.94 μM ( Fig. 7a). The fluorescence intensity at 475 nm decreased with the increase of Hg2+ ions concentration (0–1.5 mM). There was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ ions concentration (R2 = 0.987). The LOD of Hg2+ ions was calculated and verified to be 0.19 μM (Fig. 7b). Compared with the national standard detection, the hydrogel had strong fluorescence activity and could well identify Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions. Compared with conventional detection procedures, Hydrogel-based fluorescence method has widely developed and shows fascinating properties for the identification of Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions, which have recently been reported (Table S3).

To further explore the effect of other metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ag+) on the fluorescence characteristics of the hydrogel, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the hydrogel was tested (Fig. 7c). It was found that most metal ions had little interference on the hydrogel and did not quench the fluorescence like iron and mercury ions. It was demonstrated that the hydrogel could recognize Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions specifically. The anti-interference experiments were accomplished in order to prove the selection of the fluorescent probe. By means of testing the fluorescence intensity of other metal ions as interfering substances in the presence of Fe3+ ions or Hg2+ ions, it has found that hydrogel displays an outstanding anti-interference performance for the detection of Fe3+ ions or Hg2+ ions (Fig. 7d–e).

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Hg2+ or Fe3+ ions has overlap with excitation spectrum of hydrogel, as shown in Fig. 7f. The results indicate that the energy is competed with Hg2+ or Fe3+ ions, and the luminescence of hydrogel center is quenched. These results indicated that hydrogel containing cucurbit[6]uril-carbon dots had potential application value in the detection of metal ions.

The spiral position in a spiral water pipe will store dirt everywhere. These spiral positions are difficult to clean, and the detection of pollutants in these positions is also difficult. Therefore, we use shape memory hydrogel to detect the internal surface of the pipe by deformation to achieve consistency with the internal shape of the pipe. The mold of the sewer pipe was made according to the actual condition ( Fig. 8k). Soak the pipe in ferric chloride or mercury nitrate solution and rinse. The deformed hydrogel was tested on the inside of the pipeline. It was found that Fe3+ ions or Hg2+ appeared at the bend, and the fluorescence of the hydrogel was quenched under ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 8i–k).

In conclusion, we had prepared a shape memory hydrogel containing cucurbit[6]uril-carbon dots that could specifically recognize Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions. The structural stability of fluorescent probe was improved by introducing cucurbit[6]uril. This visualized gel probe facilitates the development and utilization of portable detection devices. Meanwhile, the dynamic covalent borate bonds, acylhydrazone bonds and reversible crystallization of polyvinyl alcohol chains were used to achieve the shape transformation and excellent mechanical properties of the hydrogel.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Inner Mongolia Natural Science Foundation (No. 2021MS05002).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cucurbit[7]uril–tetramethylrhodamine conjugate for direct sensing and cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. Bockus, L.C. Smith, A.G. Grice, O.A. Ali, C.C. Young, W. Mobley, A. Leek, J.L. Roberts, B. Vinciguerra, L. Isaacs, A.R. Urbach, Cucurbit[7]uril–Tetramethylrhodamine Conjugate for Direct Sensing and Cellular Imaging, J. Am. Chem. Soc., 138 (2016) 16549-16552. https://doi.org/10.1021/jacs.6b11140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new cucurbit[10]uril-based AIE fluorescent supramolecular polymer for cellular imaging
